videos.title: I Spent 72 Hours in Germany's Slums (Berlin) 🇩🇪
videos.description:
Berlin is the capital of Germany and has undergone significant changes in recent years. Waste management and a high crime rate are just two of many challenges. We spent three days in this city, exploring areas typically seen only by local residents. 

Camera: Jonas Amthor 
Project Assistant: Christian Isselbächer 
Director: Jonas Amthor
videos.transcript: We spend 3 days in the German capital, documented by a site hardly anyone knows. Poverty, litter, open drug scenes, and prostitution. I talk to the people who live here and experience firsthand how the city is changing. Welcome to Berlin from the other side. Our first steps in Berlin already paint a grim picture. We walk through streets littered with trash. Here, for example, we see a massive pile of garbage right out in the open or clothing carelessly thrown onto the street. Pigeons, rats, mice, and other animals feast on this discarded food. As we continue walking through the streets, we search for functioning trash bins. It seems we're really onto a big problem here. Finding them is a major challenge. Even when they're functional, many are overflowing and are apparently only emptied very rarely. But it's not just household waste that ends up on Berlin streets. Among bags and cans, we find some curious items. bicycles, mattresses, sofas, children's toys, drying racks, and even cars. Anyone who's done a bit of research shouldn't be surprised by these conditions. After all, Berlin is now considered one of the dirtiest cities in Europe. Some homeowners tell us that even more annoying than the trash are the graffiti scrolls. From house walls to windows, doors, and shop fronts, nothing is spared. Even the insides of building entrances have been defaced. Political messages are also sprayed across the city with paint cans. After these first impressions, we decide to explore the city center. The same picture here. Dirty sidewalks, filthy streets. Groups of homeless people often claim the sidewalks for themselves. The mental and physical state of many of these people is shocking. They stagger under the obvious influence of drugs or have turned bus stops into makeshift sleeping spots. A man, visibly drunk, sits in the middle of the sidewalk. Passers by walk around him as if he's not even there. Under a bridge, we find a cluster of tents. Here, many homeless people spend the night away from public view. A pedestrian tells us how he's experienced the changes over recent years. >> How has life here developed in recent years? Has it gotten worse? Violence has increased a bit. It's gone up. The percentage has risen. But what can I say? That's just Germany. We want to get to the bottom of the drug problem. We quickly hear that much of it is connected to Geritza Park, known in local slang as the drug park. Despite warnings, that's our next destination. On the way to Geritza Park, we notice a clear cultural shift. Many shops have Arabic signs. Palestinian flags hang in shop windows, sometimes as political statements. Shisha bars are particularly popular. Next to them, we find translation agencies or even gun shops. An Arabic influenced way of life has become part of the cityscape. The smell of marijuana hangs in the air. Some passers by smoke openly. In street cafes, people enjoy the weather. We hear at least 20 different languages. The clothing has changed, too. Many women wear headscarves or long dresses. The cultural difference is particularly striking between the few somewhat disoriented looking tourists and this way of life which has little in common with traditional western culture. The closer we get to Geritzer Park, the more vacant shops we notice, closed storefronts. Windows are boarded up, often the result of breakins. Near Gerlet Park, we suddenly hear loud shouts. Several men seem completely out of control, some even insulting passers by. >> An elderly woman rummages through a trash bin for food scraps. On the ground, we find empty nitrous oxide cartridges carelessly discarded by users. Arriving at the park, we noticed not only the numerous dealers waiting for customers, but also the massive litter problem. The entire lawns are practically covered with trash. Birds pick at their lunch among the food scraps. A local resident tells us that this isn't an exception. It's everyday life. I think it's good that you're showing it as it is so people can see. >> Berlin has always been dirty, but now tourists finally see what this city looks like. The police nowhere to be seen. Drug deals happen openly. Still, I film here secretly. I don't want to imagine what might happen if we were caught with the camera. Women who come here to get the essentials for their addiction often earn their money in the nearby red light districts in Berlin. They're part of the poverty-driven prostitution scene. You encounter them on every corner. They disappear with their clients into red lit hotel rooms. These businesses leave no customers wishes unfulfilled. While strolling further through Berlin's streets, we meet an elderly man who shares his struggles with us. >> Do you live here? >> How do you manage as a retiree? >> It's a disaster. I'm a retiree myself, but I have no chance here. It's very hard to survive. I worked in Germany for over 20 years, always paid everything, and now I can barely make ends meet. It's really terrible. We decide to take a closer look at the living conditions this elderly man endures. >> On the way, we notice a heavy police presence. A large pro Palestinian demonstration is causing road closures. There are occasional minor clashes. >> While filming, a police officer snaps at me. They don't want us documenting their work. Arriving in the residential areas, dilapidated highrises catch our eye. Many investors seem unconcerned about their condition. Tenants live in sometimes catastrophic conditions. A train passes through the neighborhood hourly, making the ground shake each time. This is where Berlin's poorest live. Building facads are covered in graffiti. Doors and buzzers look run down. Mailboxes are bent out of shape. Trash is carelessly left lying around or even thrown out of apartment windows. The retiree tells us about regular water supply outages. When that happens, a nearby fountain is his only source of water. Inside the apartments, neglect is evident at first glance. Three exhausting days in Berlin come to an end. We are left with images that cause concern. How will this city develop in the coming years? If you liked the video, subscribe to our channel. That supports our work. See you soon from a new

videos.transcript: This is a map of Berlin from 1811, and this is Berlin today. On each you can see the Spree river that runs through the city, Museum Island and Tiergarten. They're the same city, but this one is at the beginning of a massive explosion in population. And while they've both faced great destruction, for this one, the worst is yet to come. Berlin has been called “a city condemned forever to becoming and never to being”. Few cities have experienced the destruction Berlin has, but the events that scar this city are the same that have led to the vibrant Berlin of today. For the free, full version of this video and for a whole guide on Berlin, you can go to BrightTrip.com, which I have linked in the description. In this map of Berlin from 1811, you'll notice a zigzag line encompassing the center of the city. This is a remnant of a fortress wall that once surrounded Berlin. You can see Museum Island in its center. This used to be called ‘Cölln’ with its sister city, Berlin, opposite the Spree. Also, I will say I don't speak German, so pronouncing these things is really hard for me and I'm going to get it wrong. So thanks for the patients on that. In 1650 and as a response to the 30 Years War, a fortress was built surrounding both of them that eventually led to their unification. Mostly gone by 1811, you can see its lasting influence on the layout of the streets, and today, only a faint reminder of it can be seen. After the fortress, the next phase of expansion for the city is marked by a replacement wall surrounding a larger area: The Berlin Customs Wall. This wall has a much larger impact on today's map of Berlin in several ways. Not only does its route remain relatively visible, but it's from the gates in this wall that many spots in the city get their name. In German, the word gate translates to ‘Tor’. And these are all named for the places the gate or ‘tor’ led to, most famously, the one remaining gate is the Brandenburg Gate. Of this wall, only a small portion still stands and even it has been reconstructed. This is a pivotal moment in Berlin's history, because if this is 1811, look at what is about to happen in the population. This boom in population was a result of the industrial revolution, with people moving to the city to work in massive factories, some of which are still standing. Up until the 1860s this old customs wall contained a majority of the city's development. But as the population grew, so too did the city limits. This came to a head in 1920, when the suburbs were annexed, expanding Berlin by 13 times and doubling its population from 2 million to 4 million. This city boundary is essentially the same as Berlin's city state boundary of today. But amid all this growth, mass destruction awaited. Wait, it actually starts before this. As Hitler rose to power, he wanted Berlin to be the capital of an area much larger than the contemporary Germany. But he didn't really like Berlin. He called it ‘an unregulated accumulation of buildings’ and wanted to make some not so subtle changes. He planned a massive redesign of the city, followed by a name change from Berlin to Germania. The centerpiece would be a massive grand hall that could hold over 100,000 people at a time. This was to sit near where the Reichstag is and the boulevard that would lead to it would be just as monumental. 120 meters wide, it would lead to a grand arch. I sent a friend of mine, Ella, who is German and lives in Berlin, to help show us some of the things that we can't see on the map. It's really cold. I'm freezing my fingers off. I've made it to the entrance of Tiergarten. Behind me is the Brandenburg Gate. Right there. I just passed through it. And now I'm going to walk into this beautiful park. I'm supposed to be finding some curbs in the sidewalk. I haven't found them yet, but I’m gunna keep looking. These ghost curbs are essentially all that remain of the road that would have been. Everybody's walking by. Nobody's paying attention to these marks on the ground, even though they very clearly look like the curve of an old sidewalk. But then it leads to the forest. Not much of his plan was ever realized, and only a few remnants of it are visible. A Prussian military victory column was moved. The East-West Road in Tiergarten was widened, and a place where the arch would stand sits this. This is the shw.... I've just made it to the Schwerbelastungskörper. So I have made it to the entrance. And there's a sign here. It says, The Schwerbelastungskörper witness to the biggest construction project Berlin has ever seen. Planning for a gigantic project commenced in 1937 that was intended to completely redesigned Berlin. This was built to see if the ground could support the weight of the huge triumphal arch that was going to be here. It was planned to be way bigger than the Arc de Triomphe in Paris. The reason they had to test the ground is partly because of the same reason Berlin has so many lakes and is so lush. This whole area is essentially a swamp. Look at this elevation map of Berlin. It's almost perfectly flat, which can make it hard to escape the water beneath. This dense 12 and a half thousand ton structure has remained basically because the effort of dismantling it is so high. It still stands and has been described as “a silent monument to Nazi megalomania”, which I think is just a perfect description for it. But the biggest effect of this whole plan of Germania had on the city was the Nazi's destruction of much of Berlin to clear the way for it. But this destruction was soon overshadowed by what would happen during World War Two. Berlin sustained so much damage from the war that nearly 80% of its buildings and structures near the city center were destroyed. It's honestly a miracle that the Brandenburg Gate still stands at all. Just look at it in the middle of all of these ruins. This is also true of Tiergarten. In all of these maps. You've been able to see it since it's been here since the 16th century. But it almost didn't survive the war. Heavily bombed. Most trees and monuments were destroyed. Then during reconstruction, big portions of it were used for farming. Slowly, it was restored to the major piece of the city that it is. After the war ended in 1945, the city would be divided between the victors, with the Soviet Union taking the East and the allies taking the West. But it would be another 16 years in 1961, before the infamous Berlin Wall was to be built. One thing that often surprises people is that West Berlin is something of an island during the cold War. This was a microcosm of the divided country. You can see on this map that the allies controlled the western half of the country while the Soviets controlled the eastern half. This was also true of Berlin. But you'll notice that Berlin lies within the Soviet controlled region, meaning that West Berlin was fully surrounded by East Germany on all sides. This division had an immeasurable impact on the city's development. If you're unfamiliar with the Berlin Wall, it's easy to imagine it's just a simple wall. But the reality is, it's an entire security complex built by East Berlin. Initially in 1961, it began as a simple barbed wire fence. But by the time it was torn down, it was two walls, about 100 meters apart, separated by the death strip, guard towers, anti artillery obstacles and guard dogs. It took up a lot of space and its construction didn't discriminate from cemeteries or historic sites, cutting neighborhoods in half and destroying churches. But while the Wall was another point of destruction in Berlin's history, it also marked a distinct era of construction. Each side operated under different governments with different ideals, and the East especially wanted to represent their socialist ideas through their architecture. Look at Karl-Marx-Allee on the eastern side for example, named after the father of communism. This street was the center of a massive construction project to create ‘palaces for workers under socialism’. The street was made unusually wide and Frankfurter Tor was marked by two buildings reminiscent of the sister domes at Gendarmenmarkt. However, the difference between the ideology of the workers being venerated and the reality of their working conditions was pretty large. This led to an uprising in 1953 of workers striking and protesting their working quotas. West Germany supported the workers and view this as an ideological win. Even renaming the Main Street through Tiergarten. After the day of the uprising. The West experienced quite a different reconstruction because of its isolation from the rest of West Germany. Its economy didn't bounce back so quickly and new building projects were much more difficult to execute. There's actually a part of today's map of Berlin that once you see, it's hard to miss. I want to point out this because it plays into this. This is Tempelhofer Feld. For over a hundred years now, it has been used as an airfield, and it's witnessed flight demonstrations from Orville Wright and other early aviators. During the Cold War. This was a lifeline for West Berliners. Basically, everything had to be flown in from medical supplies to food to construction materials. And at one point, planes were landing every 45 seconds to drop off goods. This meant that taking on new construction projects and flying in everything was not very cost effective or feasible. This airport was in operation until 2008 when it was decommissioned, and it's now used as a park. To me, this perfectly encapsulates Berlin. It has a deep history before, through and since World War Two, and today serves as a beautiful green space with community gardens and bike routes. I know I've given a very narrow narrative of all of Berlin, and that's because I can't fit everything into a single video. But if you are interested in going to Berlin and learning more and understanding how to practically get around, we have created an entire guide about Berlin on our website, which is linked in the description below, BrightTrip.com. Huge thank you to Ella, who took the footage on the ground and helped me pronounce things the proper way. And to Ryan Balmer, who I interviewed for this, who does tours of Berlin, I'm going to link his info below. You can find his YouTube channel. And if you want to book a tour, you should do it through him. If you are interested in diving deeper into this stuff, I'm going to leave a bunch of links in the description to resources that I used. And there was one really cool interactive map that you can like see a timeline of when the buildings were built throughout Berlin. So I'll leave that all in the description and you can kind of poke around on your own. Let me know what cool stuff you find in the comments, please. I can subscribe and I'll see you in the next one. Bye.

videos.title: 18 Things I Wish I Knew Before Visiting Berlin 🇩🇪

I thought I understood Berlin. I didn’t. Not even close.

And that’s the funny thing - I even used to live here. Long enough to realise that Berlin doesn’t play by the same rules as anywhere else.

The German capital is weird, it’s brilliant, and it’ll definitely confuse you the first time you visit.

In this video, I’m sharing all the things I wish I had known before visiting Berlin: from local habits that make no sense, to insider tips that most tourists never hear about 😎

Whether it’s understanding the tipping culture, figuring out which areas are worth exploring, or realising why the city basically shuts down on Sundays - this is your honest, no-fluff guide to Berlin.

videos.transcript: I actually used to live in Berlin, so I know the city pretty well. But even after moving away and coming back recently, I realized that there are so many things about Berlin that most visitors never figure out. So here are all the things I really wish I knew before visiting Berlin. The good, the bad, and the very weird. This was honestly the first thing that shocked me when I was in Berlin a few weeks ago. Berlin used to be like most of Europe. You know, you'd round up the bill and maybe leave a euro or two if the service was good. Now, well, literally every card machine pops up with that leave a tip option and sometimes the waiter even asks you directly and it's just a bit awkward. So, you know, maybe you've had a nice chat and then suddenly it turns into this transaction. So, were you just being nice for that 5 tip or what? Some places are still cash only. You know, you'll be in a modern cafe full of laptops and oat milk, and when you go to pay, they point you to a handwritten cash only sign. So, yeah, always carry a bit of cash in Berlin and get it from a proper bank ATM because those Euronet ones, they will rob you in fees. The uban, the esban, trams, buses, they go everywhere in Berlin. And public transport here is clean, cheap, and reliable. You know, you can actually cross half the city for just a few euros. But don't forget to validate your ticket. You have to stamp it in a little yellow machine before boarding. Or you could download the Bay Fau tickets app. Also, the ticket inspectors, they dress like normal people. So, you know, just buy the ticket and chill out. Sparies are where Berlin happens. They are these tiny corner shops that sell cold beer, snacks, and just random essentials. But more importantly, they're actually social hubs. So, here in Berlin, people just grab a drink and sit outside on these traditional sparey benches. And to be honest, this is something I miss from Berlin. And when I lived there, I actually had better nights outside a sparey than I did in some bars, actually. Berliners or maybe Germans in general have completely normalized the whole walking beer thing. So you'll see people drinking on the Uban and in parks and on the streets every day. And I think it's actually gotten illegal to drink on public transport, but people do it anyways. And it's not really a rebellion. It's just about freedom. You know, they they actually trust you to be an adult in Berlin. It's such a chill, grown-up attitude compared to places where it's illegal to even hold a bottle outside. Berlin is massive, and I do mean massive. It's actually about eight times the size of Paris, and each neighborhood feels completely different. You know, you got Kitsburg, that's gritty and creative. No is chaotic and cool and prince bag is brunch and baby strollers these days but it's actually my favorite area. So don't try to see all of Berlin if you're just here for a few days. Maybe pick one or two areas per day and just explore. I used to live around here and it's still my favorite area of Berlin. There are so many nice restaurants, cafe, and bars. And unlike other parts of Berlin, there's actually quite a local feel around here. So when you're done at the Ma Park flea market, head over to this part of Prince back instead of just walking down Castan Ali with all the other tourists. That way you'll see a different side of Berlin. Berlin's club scene is iconic. There are a lot of cool things to do in Berlin, but which are actually worth your time? That's exactly what I'm covering in this video – the experiences that will give you the REAL Berlin, not just the tourist traps 🤝

Berlin is unlike any other European capital. It's not traditionally "pretty" like Paris or Rome – instead, it's raw, fascinating, and unique.

I used to live in Berlin and worked as a tour guide here, so I know this city inside and out. In this video, I'm sharing the best things to do in Berlin – from the famous landmarks everyone needs to see to the quirky local spots that most tourists completely miss 😎

Berlin's history is everywhere you look. You'll walk past remnants of the Berlin Wall, stand where East met West during the Cold War, and see how the city confronts its difficult past head-on. But it's not all heavy history – Berlin also has incredible food, amazing parks, vibrant neighbourhoods, and that famous alternative culture that makes it feel so different from the rest of Germany.

Berlin is massive, and each area has its own personality. You need to explore beyond just one neighbourhood to really understand what makes this city special. From Prenzlauer Berg to Kreuzberg to Friedrichshain – every corner tells a different story.

videos.transcript: Can you guess Berlin's most popular tourist attraction? Is it the Fernsehturm? The Reichstag dome? Or something else altogether? Berlin had more than 10 million tourists in 2022, and many visited these 12 popular attractions. Let's find out what they are. And we bet you'll never guess what came in first place! In 12th place is the Fridrichstadt-palast which was visited by 432,000 people last year. The theater is famous for its elaborate shows and its sheer size. At nearly 3,000 square meters, it boasts the world's largest theater stage. It can easily hold 100 performers, from acrobats to dancers! It's no surprise that it has been one of the city's most popular entertainment venues for over a century. The eleventh-most visited place in Berlin is the Jewish Museum. It was built by architect Daniel Libeskind and its shape is reminiscent of a broken Star of David. The interior design is symbolic, with voids of space representing the lack of jewish life after the Holocaust. The permanent exhibition recounts 1700 years of German-Jewish history which were often marked by discrimination and exclusion before culminating in the Holocaust. You'll also learn about aspects of Jewish culture, like kosher food, and what happens on Shabbat. The Jewish Museum is the first museum in our ranking, but it's not the last! At 10th place is the Kaiser Wilhelm Memorial Church a landmark in the city's west. It's not difficult to spot with its mixture of old and new architecture. The church was destroyed during World War II, but the bombed out tower was kept as a symbol of regeneration. The interior is sure to surprise you. It shines in blue and makes you feel as if you're inside an old Gothic cathedral. Take a moment to enjoy it. This plane at the German Museum of Technology is one of the so-called "raisin bombers" or "candy bombers" that supplied Berlin in 1948/49 during the Allied airlift. It's one of the many modern marvels held in this unique museum. With so much in the collection, you'll always discover something new. This hunk of machinery is the world's first computer. The Z1 was built in Berlin in 1938, by engineer Conrad Zuse. He used a vacuum cleaner motor to power it! And to make sure you don't lose your way, Robot Tim is happy to guide you through the Museum! Okay, now I'll take you to your destination. Dinosaurs may have died out millions of years ago, but they still have a large fan base, including at Berlin's Museum of Natural History. Here, you'll have plenty of opportunities to learn about plant and animal species in the museum's exhibits. And these aren't the only creatures you'll find on our ranking. Tourists in Berlin often ask how to get to the Berlin Wall. A good place to start is the memorial on Bernauer Strasse, where parts of the former border fortifications have been transformed into an open-air exhibition. You'll learn how insurmountable the Wall was, and how many people lost their lives trying to get to the West. Next on our list is Berlin's iconic Fernsehturm, or the TV tower. At 368 meters, it's the tallest structure in Germany. Take an elevator up to 203 meter to the observation floor – it takes only 40 seconds! One floor above, there's a restaurant with a rotating observation deck. From there, you have a bird's eye view over the entire city while you enjoy your meal. We hit the one-million mark with number 5. 1.4 million visitors came to the German Bundestag last year. Its glass dome is a highlight and an absolute visitor magnet! The path to the top is a spectacular spiral walkway. Although the tickets are free of charge, be sure to reserve them online in advance. One of Berlin's newest institutions already reached number 4: The Humboldt Forum. Although it may look like an historic building, it opened just two years ago and has already become a visitor favorite. The museum and exhibition space inside has lots on offer. There's art from all over the world, exhibitions about natural sciences and artefacts from Berlin's history, like the door of legendary techno club Tresor, which was kept in a bank vault! We've finally arrived at the top of our list! What do you think made it into the top 3? The Brandenburg Gate? The Holocaust Museum or perhaps the East Side Gallery? Sorry to disappoint you. Although all 3 places are popular with tourists, they aren't found in the rankings since visitor numbers to public places aren't counted. In 3rd place is the Nazi documentation center, "Topography of terror". It's a frightening name for a place where horrible events were planned and organised. From 1933 to 1945, this area near Berlin's Potsdamer Platz served as the Nazi's Gestapo, SS and Reich Security Main Office. Today, the Documentation Center provides information about perpetrators and victims, and keeps the memory of this cruel chapter of German history alive. Berlin's Museum Island, a UNESCO World Heritage Site. It's home to 5 museums that display art treasures spanning 6,000 years of human history. The Altes Museum houses a collection of antiquities. While the highlight in the Neues Museum is the bust of Queen Nefertiti. The Pergamon Museum is famous for the Pergamon Altar from ancient Greece, which is currently under renovation. And here's a tip! On the first Sunday of each month, admission to many of Berlin's museums is free, including those on Museum Island. And now, finally, number 1! More than 5 million people visited Berlin's 2 zoos last year: The Zoologischer Garten in the West and the Tierpark Friedrichsfelde in the city's east. Berlin is home to Germany's giant panda twins Pit and Paule, who were born here. A total of around 30,000 animals from 1500 species live in the 2 zoos, so if you want to see them all, it's going to take a while! Better yet, you can plan to come back again after all, Berlin is certainly worth more than one trip!

First time in Berlin?
Here’s the truth no one tells you: crowds, tourist traps, wasted hours on ticket mistakes, and incredible experiences most people miss.
But don’t worry—I’ll show you how to skip crowds, avoid traps, save time and money, and discover Berlin’s best free experiences, iconic sights, and hidden gems—all backed by my 13 years of experience as a tour guide.

💡 Pro Tips Inside:
✅ How to avoid ticket mistakes
✅ Best free experiences in Berlin
✅ How To Use Public Transport
✅ Must-see landmarks with zero regrets

videos.transcript: First time in Berlin? Here is the truth no one tells you: crowds, tourist traps, wasted hours on ticket mistakes, and incredible experiences most people miss. Stick with me, and I’ll show you exactly how to skip crowds, avoid traps, save time and money, and discover Berlin’s best free experiences - all while exploring its iconic sights and hidden gems, backed by my 13 years of experience as a tour guide. Let’s start with one of Berlin’s highlights that’s totally free, but missed by many: the rooftop of Reichstag. A stunning terrace on the top of Germany’s Parliament features a massive glass dome, where you can walk spiraling ramps with a direct view into the Parliament chamber below. Interactive displays along the way share fascinating stories about the Reichstag’s history and its role in shaping modern democracy. Here’s the catch—entry is free, but you must register in advance. Spots fill up quickly, as you can only book for the current or following month, so reserve as early as possible. You’ll find the official registration link in the description. If you didn’t register in time you can still try to book a meal at the rooftop restaurant, which grants you the same access. I’ve included the restaurant link in the description too. And as a last resort, you can try registering in person at the service center near the Reichstag. Just keep in mind, its almost impossible to get spots during busy periods. To ensure you don’t miss your time slot, arrive 15 minutes early to clear security, and don’t forget to bring your ID or passport. The Reichstag stood right on the border between East and West Berlin, much like the Brandenburg Gate, another iconic must-see landmark. This iconic symbol of Berlin is open 24/7 and completely free to visit, but no one tells you about the crowds and scams. During the post-war division of Berlin, this historic gate was on the dividing line, with the Soviets building the Berlin Wall directly in front of it. When the wall fell, the Brandenburg Gate became the ultimate symbol of reunited Berlin and Germany. Its historical significance is why it’s always crowded during peak seasons. Be cautious—it’s a hotspot for pickpockets. Keep your bag securely in front of you with zippers closed. Also avoid pushy street performers who aggressively demand tips often asking for more than expected. To enjoy the gate without the negatives, visit early in the morning or late in the evening. It’s well worth the effort as you will be rewarded with best opportunities for stunning photos. If you’re enjoying this video so far, please take a moment to hit that like button and subscribe—it really helps the channel grow and lets me share even more travel tips and useful information with you. Thank you so much! Another totally free, but often overlooked must-see is the Berlin Wall Memorial. In the 1960s East Germany constructed a guarded concrete barrier that completely encircled West Berlin to stop East Germans from fleeing to the West. The memorial preserves part of the former border strip, featuring an open-air exhibition that vividly tells the story of Berlin’s division. You’ll find original wall segments, escape tunnels, and artifacts that bring this history to life. Many overlook the Visitor Center, also free of charge, where you can find informative exhibitions. Documentation Center is another free to enter place with even more in-depth exhibits and observation platform overlooking the preserved section of the wall. But no one misses the famous Checkpoint Charlie. It’s another free landmark to visit, but no one tells you about the crowds and safety concerns. What most don’t realize is that it’s located in the middle of a busy street still used by everyday traffic. So please, keep an eye out for traffic. Another concern are actors dressed as Cold War-era soldiers - they’ll offer to pose for photos but often aggressively demand tips, asking for more than expected. Pickpockets are another concern, so keep your belongings secure at all times. If you’re only here for photos, visit early in the morning or late in the evening to avoid crowds, scammers, and traffic. And here’s something no one mentions: those “Berlin Wall fragments” sold nearby? Almost certainly fake. Speaking of fake, did you know that the entire Checkpoint Charlie is a replica? While it’s a symbol of the Cold War, it’s not the most authentic Berlin Wall experience. For a deeper understanding of Checkpoint Charlie, I highly recommend joining a Cold War-themed walking tours, and I’ll put my favorites ones in the description. These tours also often include a stop at the East Side Gallery, the longest remaining section of the Berlin Wall, transformed into the longest open-air gallery with murals created by 118 artists. Open year-round, 24/7, it’s completely free to visit. Take your time to walk the entire stretch, as each mural tells a unique story of Berlin’s history, unity, and hope. Don’t touch the murals as they’re preserved as Berlin’s cultural heritage. To avoid crowds and capture the best lighting for photos visit early or late in the afternoon. To delve deeper into the Berlin Wall’s history visit The Wall Museum adjacent to the East Side Gallery. With engaging multimedia experience and historical artifacts its provides a comprehensive understanding of the Berlin Wall. The best way to reach the East Side Gallery and other must-see sights is by using Berlin’s efficient, affordable, and reliable public transport. With the same ticket you can use the U-Bahn or metro, S-Bahn or suburban trains that connect the city center to the outskirts, trams mostly used in the former East Berlin, and buses. A Pro Tip: Instead of pricey hop-on-hop-off buses, take Public Bus Line 100, which passes many of Berlin’s iconic landmarks like the Brandenburg Gate and Berlin Cathedral. Download the BVG Ticket App to buy and validate tickets on your phone. Surprisingly, Berlin’s stations have no gates, so if you’re using a paper ticket, be sure to validate it at a machine before starting your journey as ticket inspectors conduct random checks. Transport system is divided into three fare zones. In most cases you will need a ticket for AB Zones, while Zone C ticket is needed for a ride to the Airport and Potsdam. A 24-hour pass is the easiest and most cost-effective way to explore Berlin. Another must-see is a stunning river island, home to five world-class museums. Consider the Museum Pass Card for free entry to these landmarks and many other museums in Berlin. Valid for three days, it saves you both time and money. Keep in mind: you’ll still need to book timed-entry tickets for the Neues Museum, and the Pergamon Museum is currently fully closed for extensive renovations until 2027. Despite that, the pass still offers incredible value—costing roughly the same as visiting just two museums separately. I’ll link it in the description. If you have a Student Card you can save up to 50% on admission at most attractions. Also, bring a 1 or 2-euro coin to use the lockers. Most museums require you to store bags and coats. Coin is needed to lock the locker and remove the key. You’ll get it back when you unlock it at the end of your visit. Photography is allowed in many areas of the museums but without flash. Most museums also offer excellent audio guides or apps for a more in-depth experience. Arrive early to avoid crowds, especially during peak seasons. Prioritize the museums you’re most excited about, and take breaks along the beautiful Spree River or at a nearby cafés. Right next to the five museums stands another must-see, Berlin Cathedral, the largest Protestant church in the world. In addition to religious services, the cathedral hosts over 100 concerts and events annually, so it’s worth checking the calendar in advance. To explore the interior, you’ll need a ticket linked in the description. Before entering, you’ll need to store bags, backpacks, or bulky items in lockers, so again don’t forget to bring coins . Take advantage of the provided audio guides and remember to take photos without the flash. The highlight is a climb to the cathedral’s impressive dome exposed to the elements, so check the weather beforehand. Arrive early in the morning or late in the afternoon to avoid crowds. Keep in mind that there’s no lift, and you’ll need to climb 267 steps—once you start, there’s no turning back. Nextdoor is Berlin Palace, now known as the Humboldt Forum. The royal residence of Prussian kings was heavily damaged during World War II and later demolished by East German authorities. Today, the rebuilt palace is a world-class cultural and exhibition space. Inside, you’ll find highlights like the Ethnological Museum and the Museum of Asian Art. Entry to the main building is free, with both free and ticketed exhibitions available. Be sure to check out the free exhibition that delves into the history of the palace and its significance in Berlin’s past. One of the top attractions is the rooftop terrace, offering stunning panoramic views of Berlin. The palace also features a restaurant and café, making it an excellent place to take a break while exploring the Museum Island. A short and scenic walk away is Berlin’s tallest landmark, the TV Tower. This iconic symbol of East Berlin features observation platforms and a revolving restaurant housed in its distinctive sphere, accessible via two high-speed elevators. Standing as the highest publicly accessible building in Europe, the tower offers unbeatable 360-degree views of Berlin. For the best experience, check the weather forecast as clear days provide the most stunning views. At the observation deck check interactive panels that help identify Berlin’s landmarks while sharing fascinating historical context. Visit early in the morning or during sunset for the best light and fewer crowds. As the TV tower is one of the most popular attractions you should book your tickets well ahead, so be sure to check the description below. Tower is located in Alexanderplatz, one of the city’s busiest transport hubs and the heart of East Berlin. This lively area is perfect for shopping, dining, or grabbing a coffee. While you’re here, don’t miss the chance to grab Currywurst, a must-try Berlin street food experience. Invented in the post-war era, Currywurst came to life when a sliced pork sausage was topped with ketchup and curry powder brought over by British soldiers. Currywurst is typically served in a small paper tray with a wooden fork and is often accompanied by fries or a bread roll. Unfortunately many miss Berlin’s incredible royal heritage, as the city was once the royal capital of Germany. A must-see is Charlottenburg Palace, an impressive summer residence that offers a fascinating glimpse into the grandeur of Prussian royalty. The highlight is the Old Palace, a stunning example of Baroque architecture, featuring opulent rooms decorated with lavish details and exquisite art collections. Before entering, you’ll need to store bags, backpacks, or bulky items in lockers, so make sure to bring a 2-euro coin to lock your belongings. Photography is allowed in most parts, just remember to turn off your flash. While the gardens themselves are free to explore, the Basic Ticket Grants you access to the Old Palace. Combined Ticket adds the New Wing, and pavilions within the Gardens. Wear comfortable shoes, as exploring the palace and its gardens involves a fair bit of walking. If you visit in the summer, bring a water bottle to stay hydrated. Early morning or late afternoons are best to avoid crowds. But the highlight of royal heritage is on the outskirts of Berlin in a town of Potsdam. With massive parks and several palaces, it has the highest concentration of UNESCO World Heritage Sites in Germany. The gardens are free to explore, but entry to the palaces requires tickets. To save time and money consider a combined ticket with access to multiple palaces. Guided tours might be another great idea to cover as much as possible in most efficient way while providing fascinating information. Be sure to book tickets and tours in advance, so check the links in the description. Potsdam is easily accessible by S-Bahn Line S7, taking just 40 minutes. An ABC ticket covers the entire journey, including local buses and trams within Potsdam. Plan at least half a day, wear comfortable shoes and pack a water bottle. Your first time in Berlin doesn’t have to be overwhelming. Follow these tips, and Berlin’s rich history and vibrant culture will leave you with unforgettable memories and no regrets. I’ve linked all the tickets and tours mentioned in the description and the QR code on the screen, so don’t miss out! Let me know in the comments—what’s the biggest travel scam you’ve encountered? My name is Rok - thanks for watching, and I’ll see you next time!
